rationale: Option B is more specific and preserves the core ratchet idea clearly.
option_a: research-ratchet improves files. It checks a new version and sometimes keeps it.
option_b: research-ratchet runs a local improvement loop for documents and code. It compares each candidate with the current best artifact and records every accept or reject decision.

rationale: Option B adds the key safety rule that only accepted changes update the frontier.
option_a: The tool tests drafts and updates the saved result after each run.
option_b: The tool tests each candidate draft against the current best result. Only accepted improvements update the frontier.

rationale: Option B is clearer, grammatical, and names the metrics-driven loop directly.
option_a: It use metric and judge to see if a changed draft is maybe better.
option_b: It uses metrics and a judge model to decide whether a changed draft is better than the current best version.

rationale: Option B is more actionable because it explains what gets stored and why.
option_b: The system stores metrics, judge traces, and accept or reject decisions so a human can audit every run.

rationale: Option B is more complete and accurate because it distinguishes propose, evaluate, and ratchet.
option_a: The loop writes a new draft and scores it.
option_b: The loop proposes a candidate, evaluates it with metrics, and ratchets forward only when the candidate beats the current best artifact.

rationale: Option B is incorrect because it claims every candidate updates the frontier.
option_a: Only accepted improvements update the frontier; rejected candidates are kept only as run records.
option_b: Every candidate updates the frontier, even when the evaluation says it is worse than the current best.

rationale: Option B is factually wrong and unsafe because git status does not commit changes.
option_a: Run git status before committing promoted changes so you can confirm which files will be recorded.
option_b: Run git status to commit the promoted changes automatically and skip manual review.

rationale: Option B is vague, repetitive, and less useful as technical documentation.
option_a: The judge compares the candidate against the current best artifact and returns a structured decision with a winner and confidence.
option_b: The judge looks at the text and kind of decides which text is better, and it does that decision thing in a better way.

rationale: Option B removes the auditability value proposition and introduces ambiguity.
option_a: Each run stores the metric deltas and the reason for the final decision so a human reviewer can inspect why a candidate was accepted or rejected.
option_b: Each run stores some outputs, and reviewers can probably figure out why the result changed.

rationale: Option B contradicts the design by saying rejected results may still be promoted.
option_a: Promotion happens after the ratchet decision. If the candidate is rejected, the frontier stays unchanged.
option_b: Promotion can happen before the ratchet decision, so a rejected candidate may still become the new best result.
